segments.text: In this lesson I will show a simple git workflow for a solo developer. We will cover clone, branch, commit, and push with one small example repository.
Now I create a feature branch, make a small change, inspect git status, and explain when to commit versus when to amend a local change before pushing.
Finally I open a pull request, explain why frequent small commits are easier to review, and recap the everyday commands a beginner should remember.
Today we derive gradient descent for linear models, then connect that optimization view to representation learning and generalization in deep neural networks.
The lecture assumes comfort with calculus, matrix multiplication, vector spaces, bias variance tradeoffs, and supervised learning objectives.
We compare logistic regression with multilayer networks, discuss loss landscapes, regularization, and why optimization difficulty changes with model capacity.
The closing section reviews practical training failure modes such as vanishing gradients, unstable learning rates, and overfitting under small data conditions.
This talk explains how to keep reading notes useful over time by writing small summaries, linking related ideas, and revisiting notes before they decay.
The speaker compares passive bookmarking with active note making, then gives examples of lightweight capture, weekly review, and tagging around themes.
The final section argues that a note system should support retrieval, reflection, and future project work rather than storing raw highlights with no context.
SQLite is a small embedded database. In this quick overview I explain when it fits a local app, what a table is, and why a single file database is convenient.
We briefly touch schemas, indexes, and a simple content items table, but this is an introductory overview rather than a deep performance tuning guide.
